Since the tool `skills-ref` will be not available for most of the users, can we build our internal nodejs skills-ref version (our built-in tool can have a better name if you think it will be a good idea) that will replace the python versoin and being available/bundled when the user install the module? This way, we will always be sure that, skills-ref node command will be availabe. Here is the full source code moved in @_bmad-output/agentskills/skills-ref.

task:
This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   The user invoked the `/bmad-bmb-edit-module` workflow to edit the Skill Forge module (`src/`). Their primary concern was that skills generated by the quick-skill workflow lacked YAML frontmatter required by the agentskills.io specification, causing `npx skills add` to fail with "No valid skills found. Skills require a SKILL.md with name and description." They wanted to:
   - Identify the best place/phase to enforce skill compliance
   - Determine if `skills-ref validate` from agentskills.io was ever actually checked
   - Fix all skill generation workflows (quick-skill, create-skill, create-stack-skill) and the test-skill workflow to enforce frontmatter compliance
   - Build a built-in Node.js replacement for the Python `skills-ref` tool so users don't need to install Python dependencies

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - **agentskills.io specification**: SKILL.md must have YAML frontmatter with `name` and `description` fields. Only 6 frontmatter fields are permitted: `name`, `description`, `license`, `compatibility`, `metadata`, `allowed-tools`. Unknown fields cause validation failure.
   - **Name constraints**: 1-64 chars, lowercase alphanumeric + hyphens only, must match parent directory name, no leading/trailing hyphens, no consecutive hyphens, NFKC Unicode normalization applied.
   - **Description constraints**: 1-1024 chars, non-empty, trigger-optimized for agent discovery.
   - **Progressive disclosure**: frontmatter (~100 tokens) loads at discovery, full SKILL.md (<5000 tokens) at activation, resources on demand.
   - **Forge tiers**: Quick (no tools), Forge (ast-grep), Deep (ast-grep + gh + qmd) — each has different skill generation workflows.
   - **BMAD step-file workflow architecture**: Micro-file design with just-in-time loading, sequential enforcement, numbered s
from what we did from this claude session, do we need to update @README.md , @docs/ or @_bmad-output/planning-artifacts/medium-article-skf.md?
I am just curious how the built-in tool will be called and used.

commit the updated docs. Next, from what we did before (use the memory plugin), we got an issue when we tried to install our module with just `skill-forge` keywords because npm resolve package by name. Are you sure we won't face the same issue with `npx skill-check`? Check the @docs/getting-started.md for the different installation methods.
user_feedback_text: commit the updated docs. Next, from what we did before (use the memory plugin), we got an issue when we tried to install our module with just `skill-forge` keywords because npm resolve package by name. Are you sure we won't face the same issue with `npx skill-check`? Check the @docs/getting-started.md for the different installation methods.

task: oh wait! I did know we have an existing project that serves to check skill. Here is the repo of the project your mentioned: https://github.com/thedaviddias/skill-check. Its readme file says everything. It is more mature implementation rather than our built-in tool. May be we should take the full advantage of this tool instead on relying on our internal and poor tool. what do you think? May be we should completely drop the commit related to adding ot the built-in tool (here is the revision number: 99dbc30f3de526c43e3853cff68b6f39c5f77807)
